title: Philemon 1
subtitle: A Brother, No Longer a Slave

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — "Your Love Has Given Me Great Joy"

heb.paragraph: The noun splanchna (vv. 7, 12, 20) literally denotes the internal organs (heart, lungs, liver)—the seat of deep emotion in ancient physiology. Paul uses it three times in this brief letter, expressing the visceral depth of Christian affection. Philemon has ‘refreshed the hearts’ (ta splanchna) of the saints (v. 7); Paul sends Onesimus, who is ‘my very heart’ (ta ema splanchna, v. 12); and Paul asks Philemon to ‘refresh my heart’ (v. 20).

refs.note: The mentions of Onesimus and Archippus in Colossians connect the two letters and confirm they were sent simultaneously to the same community.
Paul’s greetings to Prisca, Aquila, and ‘the church that meets at their house’ provide a parallel to the house-church setting of Philemon.

notes.note: Paul’s self-designation as ‘prisoner’ rather than ‘apostle’ sets the rhetorical tone: this is a personal appeal, not an apostolic command. MacArthur notes that the community address (Apphia, Archippus, the whole church) ensures accountability: Philemon’s response will be public.

The thanksgiving is not flattery but strategic praise: by celebrating Philemon’s character (love, faith, generosity), Paul creates a moral framework within which the coming request becomes nearly impossible to refuse. If Philemon is the man Paul describes, he will do what Paul asks.

Calvin notes the rhetorical artistry: Paul begins with praise in order to persuade. But the praise is genuine: Philemon’s love and faith are real. Calvin observes that the ‘sharing of faith’ (koinōnia tēs pisteōs, v. 6) means not evangelism but the active, practical expression of faith in love for the saints.

The phrase hē koinōnia tēs pisteōs sou (the sharing/fellowship of your faith) is one of the most debated clauses in the letter. The NET translates it as ‘the faith you share with us,’ reading koinōnia as the common faith shared among believers.

The verb anapaueō (to refresh, give rest) will recur in v. 20, creating an inclusio: Philemon has refreshed others’ hearts; Paul asks him to refresh his. The perfect tense eschēka (I have had) indicates a past event with continuing effect.

Bruce identifies the letter’s rhetorical genre as a letter of recommendation and intercession, following established Greco-Roman conventions (cf. Pliny, Ep. 9.21). Paul adapts the form by introducing the christological dimension: the relationship between Philemon, Onesimus, and Paul is reconfigured by the gospel.

O’Brien reads the thanksgiving as carefully preparing the ground for the appeal. Every detail is relevant: Philemon’s love (v. 5) anticipates the request for love toward Onesimus; his refreshing of the saints’ hearts (v. 7) anticipates the request to refresh Paul’s heart (v. 20). The rhetorical skill is extraordinary: Paul positions the appeal as the natural extension of virtues Philemon already possesses.

hist.context: Philemon opens with Paul identifying himself as ‘a prisoner of Christ Jesus’ (v. 1)—not ‘an apostle’—striking a tone of appeal rather than authority. The letter is addressed to Philemon (‘our dear friend and fellow worker,’ v. 1), Apphia (‘our sister,’ v. 2), Archippus (‘our fellow soldier,’ v. 2), and the church that meets in Philemon’s house (v. 2). The co-addressees ensure that the appeal is not merely private but has the community as witness. The thanksgiving (vv. 4–7) is strategically crafted: Paul praises Philemon’s love and faith, his ‘sharing of your faith’ (koinōnia, v. 6), and the joy his love has brought. The praise establishes the relational context for the request that follows: Philemon is the kind of person who refreshes hearts (v. 7)—will he refresh Paul’s?

sections.header: Verses 8–16 — "I Appeal to You for My Son Onesimus"

heb.paragraph: The verb parakaleō (v. 10) introduces the letter’s central request. Paul deliberately chooses appeal (parakalō) over command (epitassō, v. 8): though he has the apostolic authority to order Philemon, he prefers to persuade through love. The choice transforms the letter from a directive into a negotiation between equals in Christ.

The wordplay on Onesimus’s name (‘Useful’) in v. 11 is a pun with theological significance: Onesimus, whose name means ‘useful,’ was formerly ‘useless’ (achrēstos) to Philemon but is now ‘useful’ (euchrēstos) to both. The transformation from useless to useful embodies the gospel’s power to make all things new.

refs.note: Paul’s teaching that slaves are ‘the Lord’s freed people’ and free persons are ‘Christ’s slaves’ provides the theological framework for the reconfiguration of Onesimus’s status.
Paul’s declaration that in Christ ‘there is neither slave nor free’ finds its most concrete application in the letter to Philemon, where a specific slave is to be received as a brother.

notes.note: Paul’s choice of appeal over command is deliberate: he wants Philemon’s action to be voluntary, not coerced. MacArthur notes the theological principle: grace-motivated obedience is more valuable than compelled compliance. The description of Onesimus as Paul’s ‘son’ (teknon) implies spiritual paternity through the gospel.

The name pun (useless/useful) captures the gospel’s transformative power. Paul would prefer to keep Onesimus but sends him back so that Philemon’s ‘favour might not seem forced, but voluntary’ (v. 14). MacArthur notes the ethical principle: even a good outcome achieved through coercion lacks moral value.

The suggestion that Onesimus’s departure was providentially ordered transforms the narrative from crime to comedy: what Onesimus intended as flight, God intended as mission. The request—‘no longer as a slave, but as a dear brother’—is revolutionary: Paul asks Philemon to treat his slave as his spiritual equal, ‘both as a fellow man and as a brother in the Lord.’

Calvin reads the entire appeal as a model of Christian persuasion: Paul uses reason, affection, humour (the name pun), providence, and the gospel’s logic to transform Philemon’s understanding of the situation. Calvin notes that Paul does not directly command manumission but makes it nearly impossible to avoid: if Onesimus is a ‘dear brother,’ how can he remain mere property?

The verb egennēsa (I became the father of) indicates that Paul led Onesimus to faith during his imprisonment. The phrase en tois desmois (in my chains) emphasises the irony: a prisoner gives birth to a new spiritual life.

The temporal contrast hōran (for a time/moment) versus aiōnion (forever) suggests that the brief separation may result in a permanent transformation of the relationship. The NET notes that aiōnion may mean ‘for life’ or ‘for eternity.’

Bruce provides extensive comparison with Pliny’s letter to Sabinianus (Ep. 9.21), in which Pliny intercedes for a freedman who has offended his patron. Paul’s letter goes far beyond Pliny’s: Pliny asks for clemency; Paul asks for brotherhood. Bruce notes that the theological reconfiguration of the master-slave relationship—from property to person, from slave to brother—is the logical application of the baptismal declaration of Gal 3:28.

O’Brien identifies the rhetorical escalation: the appeal moves from authority Paul could exercise (v. 8) to affection he actually deploys (v. 9), from Onesimus’s new identity (vv. 10–11) to God’s providential purpose (v. 15), culminating in the radical request: receive him as a brother (v. 16). Each step makes refusal more difficult while preserving Philemon’s dignity and freedom of choice.

hist.context: The heart of the letter. Paul appeals to Philemon on behalf of Onesimus, whom he describes as ‘my son’ (v. 10)—a spiritual child born during Paul’s imprisonment. The appeal is structured as a carefully escalating series of arguments: (1) Paul has the right to command but prefers to appeal (vv. 8–9); (2) Onesimus is now Paul’s son in the faith (v. 10); (3) Onesimus is now genuinely useful (v. 11); (4) Paul sends Onesimus back, though he would rather keep him (vv. 12–14); (5) perhaps Onesimus’s departure was providentially ordered so that Philemon could receive him back permanently—‘no longer as a slave, but better than a slave, as a dear brother’ (vv. 15–16). The request is not merely for lenient treatment but for a fundamental reconception of the relationship: from property to family.

sections.header: Verses 17–25 — "Welcome Him as You Would Welcome Me"

heb.paragraph: The noun koinōnos (v. 17) is the basis of Paul’s climactic appeal: ‘if you consider me a partner (koinōnon), welcome him as you would welcome me.’ The term implies shared participation in the gospel’s benefits and obligations. If Philemon and Paul are partners, then what Paul values (Onesimus) Philemon must also value. The partnership creates a relational logic that compels reception.

refs.note: The substitutionary language of Phlm 18–19—‘charge it to me’—echoes the Servant’s bearing of others’ iniquities. Paul models the gospel pattern of imputation: assuming another’s debt.
Paul’s ministry of reconciliation—God reconciling the world through Christ—finds its most concrete expression in Philemon, where Paul mediates reconciliation between Philemon and Onesimus.

notes.note: Paul’s offer to pay Onesimus’s debt is the gospel in miniature: a mediator assumes the offender’s obligation and offers his own credit. MacArthur draws the parallel to Christ: as Paul says ‘charge it to me,’ so Christ says to the Father on behalf of sinners, ‘charge it to my account.’ The subtle reminder (‘you owe me your very self’) deftly tilts the balance: the debt Philemon owes Paul (his salvation) far exceeds anything Onesimus owes Philemon.

Paul’s confidence that Philemon will ‘do even more than I ask’ has been interpreted as a request for manumission, for Onesimus’s return to Paul’s service, or simply for generous treatment. MacArthur notes the deliberate ambiguity: Paul lets the gospel’s logic work on Philemon’s conscience.

The announcement of a planned visit is both encouraging (Paul expects release) and motivating (Philemon knows Paul will see the outcome in person). The greetings match Colossians 4, confirming the letters’ simultaneous dispatch.

Calvin reads the substitutionary offer as a deliberate theological model: Paul acts as Christ’s stand-in, absorbing the sinner’s debt. Calvin notes the irony: Paul, a prisoner with no apparent means, offers to pay a debt that Philemon, a wealthy man, is owed. The moral pressure is enormous: if Paul, who has nothing, offers to pay, how can Philemon, who has everything, refuse to forgive?

Calvin reads ‘more than I ask’ as an invitation to manumission: the gospel’s logic, once fully embraced, cannot stop at mere clemency but must reach liberation. Calvin insists that while Paul does not command manumission, the letter’s theology makes any other outcome morally incoherent.

The phrase ei de ti ēdikēsen se ē opheilei (if he has wronged you or owes anything) uses a first-class condition (ei + indicative), treating the possibility as real. The NET notes that the verb adikeō may refer to the offence of running away, theft, or both.

Paul’s autograph (egō Paulos egrapsa tē emē cheiri) functions as a legal IOU. The phrase hina mē legō soi hoti kai seauton moi prosópheileis (not to mention that you owe me your very self) is a classical example of paralipsis: mentioning something while claiming not to.

Bruce identifies Paul’s substitutionary offer as the letter’s theological climax: the apostle models the gospel he preaches. Bruce notes that ancient letters of intercession typically asked for clemency; Paul goes further by assuming the offender’s liability and asking for brotherhood. Bruce reads the ‘more than I ask’ as almost certainly implying manumission: the logic of brotherhood is incompatible with ownership.

O’Brien reads the conclusion as the culmination of the letter’s rhetorical strategy. The partnership appeal (v. 17), the debt assumption (vv. 18–19), the reciprocal obligation (v. 19b), and the confidence in compliance (v. 21) together create an appeal that is almost impossible to refuse. O’Brien notes that the visit announcement (v. 22) adds a practical dimension to the appeal: Philemon’s response will not remain private but will be visible to the apostle himself.

hist.context: The letter’s climax. Paul makes the decisive request: ‘welcome him as you would welcome me’ (v. 17). He then removes the financial obstacle: ‘if he has done you any wrong or owes you anything, charge it to me’ (v. 18). Paul’s autograph IOU (‘I, Paul, am writing this with my own hand. I will pay it back,’ v. 19a) is legally binding but accompanied by a delicate reminder: ‘not to mention that you owe me your very self’ (v. 19b). The closing expresses confidence that Philemon will ‘do even more than I ask’ (v. 21)—a tantalising hint that Paul may be hoping for manumission. Paul announces a planned visit (v. 22)—adding the prospect of personal accountability. Greetings from five co-workers (identical to Col 4:10–14) and a grace benediction close the letter.

themes.note: Mercy dominates as the letter is an extended appeal for gracious reception. Justice is high through the debt-assumption and the radical equality demanded by the gospel. Covenant appears through the partnership language. Mission is prominent as reconciliation embodies the gospel’s social transformative power.

rows.label: vv.1–3
rows.text: Salutation: prisoner, fellow worker, house church

rows.label: vv.8–10
rows.text: Appeal: authority set aside; Onesimus as Paul’s child

rows.label: vv.11–14
rows.text: Useful not useless; sending back so the favour is voluntary

rows.label: vv.15–16
rows.text: Providential purpose: no longer a slave but a dear brother

rows.label: vv.17–19
rows.text: Welcome him as me; charge his debt to my account

rows.label: vv.20–21
rows.text: Refresh my heart; confidence in ‘more than I ask’

lit.note: Philemon is a masterpiece of persuasion in 25 verses: Paul moves from praise (vv. 1–7) through appeal (vv. 8–16) to commitment (vv. 17–25), deploying affection, theology, humour, and financial guarantee to transform a master-slave relationship into a brotherhood. The letter is the gospel in microcosm: a mediator assumes the offender’s debt and secures reconciliation.

hebtext.note: The noun splanchna (vv. 7, 12, 20) literally denotes the internal organs (heart, lungs, liver)—the seat of deep emotion in ancient physiology. Paul uses it three times in this brief letter, expressing the visceral depth of Christian affection. Philemon has ‘refreshed the hearts’ (ta splanchna) of the saints (v. 7); Paul sends Onesimus, who is ‘my very heart’ (ta ema splanchna, v. 12); and Paul asks Philemon to ‘refresh my heart’ (v. 20).

The verb parakaleō (v. 10) introduces the letter’s central request. Paul deliberately chooses appeal (parakalō) over command (epitassō, v. 8): though he has the apostolic authority to order Philemon, he prefers to persuade through love. The choice transforms the letter from a directive into a negotiation between equals in Christ.

The wordplay on Onesimus’s name (‘Useful’) in v. 11 is a pun with theological significance: Onesimus, whose name means ‘useful,’ was formerly ‘useless’ (achrēstos) to Philemon but is now ‘useful’ (euchrēstos) to both. The transformation from useless to useful embodies the gospel’s power to make all things new.

The noun koinōnos (v. 17) is the basis of Paul’s climactic appeal: ‘if you consider me a partner (koinōnon), welcome him as you would welcome me.’ The term implies shared participation in the gospel’s benefits and obligations. If Philemon and Paul are partners, then what Paul values (Onesimus) Philemon must also value. The partnership creates a relational logic that compels reception.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in A Brother, No Longer a Slave

translations.text: yet I prefer to appeal to you on the basis of love. It is as none other than Paul—an old man and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus—
yet for love’s sake I prefer to appeal to you—I, Paul, an old man and now a prisoner also for Christ Jesus—
because I hear about your love for all his holy people and your faith in the Lord Jesus.
because I hear of your love and of the faith that you have toward the Lord Jesus and for all the saints,

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads A Brother, No Longer a Slave within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
